segments.text: Do you know traffic lights were not always red, yellow and green? The original third color was white and that white caused so many deaths. Let's
understand why were they designed like that in the first place. In 1840s,
railroads used red for stop, green for caution, and white for go. A simple system that worked perfectly for decades. But one night in 1914,
everything went wrong. What happened next has changed the transportation forever. A red lens cracked and fell out of a railway signal. The white bulb
behind it was now exposed. Because of that, the train engineer saw the white light. He thought it meant go, which led to a collision and people died. Here is
what went wrong. Railway signals used color glass in front of white bulbs.
When the glass broke, the red became white and then a disaster. But there was even a bigger problem. At night, white signals looked exactly like the stars in
the sky, which made tracking so difficult. So this sacrifice led engineers to change white to yellow. No star looked yellow, and yellow finally
meant caution. You see, every traffic light follows this rule. For more why behind design subscribe.
